metadata.description: Complete scoring function definitions for 115 PRISM risk events. Each event has 3 sub-probabilities (P_preconditions, P_trigger, P_implementation), each with 3-4 observable indicators, normalization functions, and weights.

normalization_types: discrete_map: lookup table mapping specific values to 0-1

dynamic_scoring_functions.scoring_functions.p_preconditions: Dynamic scoring function to compute P(preconditions) from indicators
dynamic_scoring_functions.scoring_functions.p_trigger: Dynamic scoring function to compute P(trigger) from indicators
dynamic_scoring_functions.scoring_functions.p_implementation: Dynamic scoring function to compute P(implementation) from indicators

field_mapping.note: The developer should use scoring_functions for dynamic recalculation. The static p_pre/p_trig/p_impl values serve as calibration baselines.

China.basis: Alibaba Cloud 34% domestic share. Data localization forces domestic providers. Switching between Chinese/international clouds effectively prohibited.

SE_Asia.basis: Growing cloud market with all 3 hyperscalers present. Singapore data center hub reduces latency concerns.

India.basis: India data localization (RBI mandates) creates partial lock-in. But multi-cloud adoption growing. AWS, Azure, GCP all expanding India regions.

EU.basis: EU Data Act (2023/2854): mandates switching rights for cloud services. GDPR portability Art 20. Regulatory protection against lock-in strongest globally.

LATAM.basis: Brazil LGPD. Limited local cloud infrastructure forces hyperscaler dependency. São Paulo region: all 3 providers present.

Africa.basis: Limited local data centers. South Africa: AWS, Azure Cape Town. Rest of continent: latency to EU/US regions. Deep lock-in once committed.

geographic_adjustment.data_quality_note: Event-specific regional adjustments based on infrastructure, regulatory, and market data cited in each basis field.

correlation.mechanism: High vendor lock-in (61% single-provider dependency, IDC 2024) creates pricing power. Customers cannot credibly threaten to switch. Broadcom VMware (2024) demonstrated: market-dominant vendor imposed 300-1200% price increases targeting locked-in customers.
correlation.mathematical_relationship: Multiplier = 1.0 + (concentration_score × 0.35) = 1.0 + (0.72 × 0.35) = 1.252. AWS+Azure+GCP HHI≈3200 (Synergy 2025). Switching cost: 16.8 months avg migration (Flexera 2025). Broadcom exploitation: 300-1200% price hikes post-acquisition.
correlation.note: Independence assumption would underestimate by ~25.2%. Prior under independence: 0.1278 vs conditional: 0.1601.
correlation.concentration_rationale: AWS+Azure+GCP HHI≈3200 (Synergy 2025). Switching cost: 16.8 months avg migration (Flexera 2025). Broadcom exploitation: 300-1200% price hikes post-acquisition.

cascade_participation.note: Not part of documented cascade chains. Use unconditional probability.

China.basis: Alibaba Cloud, Tencent Cloud domestic. Government-backed redundancy investments. But less transparent incident reporting.

SE_Asia.basis: Singapore hub provides regional resilience. All major providers have Singapore AZs. Regional redundancy adequate.

India.basis: Mumbai, Hyderabad regions from AWS/Azure. Single-region deployments common, increasing outage impact.

EU.basis: Multiple availability zones across EU. GDPR encourages multi-region deployment. Regulatory-driven resilience.

LATAM.basis: São Paulo region concentration. Limited secondary regions for failover. AWS São Paulo, Azure Brazil South.

Africa.basis: AWS Cape Town, Azure South Africa. Limited AZ diversity. Submarine cable cuts can isolate continent.

geographic_adjustment.data_quality_note: Event-specific regional adjustments based on infrastructure, regulatory, and market data cited in each basis field.

correlation.note: Cloud outages are driven by cascading infrastructure failures (hardware fault, software bug, human error). Uptime Institute 2024: outage frequency shows no correlation with provider market share — AWS, Azure, GCP have similar outage rates per TB served despite different market shares. P(trigger) is driven by system complexity, not market structure.

tail_risk.note: Multi-region cloud outage: CrowdStrike Jul 2024 (8.5M devices) demonstrated single-point-of-failure at infrastructure layer. AI workload concentration on few providers creates novel systemic risk.

cascade_participation.note: Not part of documented cascade chains. Use unconditional probability.

DIG-CLS-003.event_name: Cloud pricing shock / Cost escalation (>30% YoY)

China.basis: Domestic cloud pricing less transparent. Government-directed pricing possible. Enterprise negotiations opaque.

SE_Asia.basis: Competitive market with all providers present. Price competition active in Singapore hub.

India.basis: Growing market with aggressive pricing from all 3 hyperscalers. But rupee depreciation amplifies USD-denominated costs.

EU.basis: EU Digital Markets Act constrains pricing behavior for gatekeepers. Competition enforcement active. EUCS certification upcoming.

LATAM.basis: USD-denominated pricing creates FX exposure. Local currency depreciation amplifies cloud costs.

Africa.basis: Premium pricing for African regions. Limited competition. USD denomination + ZAR/NGN volatility.

geographic_adjustment.data_quality_note: Event-specific regional adjustments based on infrastructure, regulatory, and market data cited in each basis field.

correlation.mechanism: Cloud market concentration (AWS/Azure/GCP ~70% share, Gartner 2024) enables coordinated pricing behavior. When one provider raises prices, others follow within 6-12 months. Customers locked into specific cloud architectures face switching costs of 6-24 months.
correlation.mathematical_relationship: Multiplier = 1.0 + (concentration_score × 0.35) = 1.0 + (0.58 × 0.35) = 1.203. Top-3 cloud ~70% share (HHI≈2200). But multi-cloud growing: Flexera 2025 reports 86% pursuing multi-cloud, partially limiting pricing power. Pricing coordination less aggressive than vendor lock-in.
correlation.note: Independence assumption would underestimate by ~20.3%. Prior under independence: 0.0804 vs conditional: 0.0967.
correlation.concentration_rationale: Top-3 cloud ~70% share (HHI≈2200). But multi-cloud growing: Flexera 2025 reports 86% pursuing multi-cloud, partially limiting pricing power. Pricing coordination less aggressive than vendor lock-in.

cascade_participation.note: Not part of documented cascade chains. Use unconditional probability.

China.basis: China Cybersecurity Law + PIPL + Data Security Law = most complex data residency requirements. Cross-border data transfer near-impossible.

SE_Asia.basis: PDPA (Singapore, Thailand). Vietnam Decree 13. Data localization requirements growing but implementation varies.

India.basis: India DPDP Act 2023. RBI data localization for financial data. Growing but not yet as restrictive as China/EU.

EU.basis: GDPR Schrems II: strictest cross-border transfer rules. But well-understood compliance pathways (SCCs, BCRs, DPF).

LATAM.basis: Brazil LGPD, Argentina PDPA. Data localization emerging but frameworks less mature than EU/China.

Africa.basis: South Africa POPIA, Kenya DPA. Nigerian NDPR. Fragmented requirements across 54 jurisdictions.

geographic_adjustment.data_quality_note: Event-specific regional adjustments based on infrastructure, regulatory, and market data cited in each basis field.

correlation.note: Data sovereignty compliance failure depends on regulatory enforcement timing (GDPR, PIPL, PDPA) which is driven by political/enforcement cycles, not by P(pre) market penetration. Max Schrems decisions have come at irregular intervals (2015, 2020) unrelated to cloud adoption rates.

cascade_participation.note: Not part of documented cascade chains. Use unconditional probability.

China.basis: API ecosystem more fragmented (WeChat, Alibaba, Baidu vs Western APIs). Platform policy changes sudden and politically motivated.

SE_Asia.basis: API ecosystem follows global patterns. Grab, Shopee APIs similar deprecation risk to global platforms.

India.basis: India Stack (Aadhaar, UPI) APIs generally stable. Private platform APIs follow global patterns.

EU.basis: EU Platform Regulation provides API stability requirements. Open Banking PSD2/PSD3 mandates maintained APIs.

LATAM.basis: PIX (Brazil), SPEI (Mexico) provide stable financial APIs. Private platform APIs follow global patterns.

Africa.basis: M-Pesa API ecosystem stable but limited. Fragmented digital infrastructure creates integration complexity.

geographic_adjustment.data_quality_note: Event-specific regional adjustments based on infrastructure, regulatory, and market data cited in each basis field.

correlation.mechanism: API-first architecture proliferation (82% of organizations, Postman 2025) increases dependency on third-party platforms. Deeper integration = higher switching costs when deprecation occurs.
correlation.mathematical_relationship: Multiplier = 1.0 + (concentration_score × 0.35) = 1.0 + (0.42 × 0.35) = 1.147. API dependency broad but alternatives usually exist. Postman 2025: 82% API-first but microservice architecture allows partial substitution. Twitter/X case (2023) showed impact concentrated on single-platform dependents.
correlation.note: Independence assumption would underestimate by ~14.7%. Prior under independence: 0.1376 vs conditional: 0.1578.
correlation.concentration_rationale: API dependency broad but alternatives usually exist. Postman 2025: 82% API-first but microservice architecture allows partial substitution. Twitter/X case (2023) showed impact concentrated on single-platform dependents.

DIG-CLS-006.event_name: National cloud / Data center capacity shortage

North_America.basis: Largest data center market globally. Virginia: 35%+ of global hyperscale. Power constraints but alternatives available.

China.basis: Data localization mandates. Alibaba, Tencent, Huawei domestic providers. Government approval required for expansion. Different capacity dynamics.

SE_Asia.basis: Singapore: moratorium on new data centers (2019-2022, now green-only). Jakarta, Bangkok: power constraints limiting expansion.

India.basis: Mumbai, Chennai data center hubs growing rapidly. Power availability improving. Land acquisition and water cooling challenges.

LATAM.basis: São Paulo, Santiago hubs developing. Power reliability concerns in secondary markets. Limited edge infrastructure.

Africa.basis: Data center capacity severely limited. South Africa (Johannesburg), Kenya (Nairobi) only meaningful markets. Submarine cable dependency.

geographic_adjustment.data_quality_note: Event-specific regional adjustments based on infrastructure, regulatory, and market data cited in each basis field.

correlation.mechanism: Data center capacity constraints (vacancy rates <5% in major markets, CBRE 2024) mean any demand surge or facility failure has amplified impact. Power grid constraints (Northern Virginia, Singapore) limit expansion.
correlation.mathematical_relationship: Multiplier = 1.0 + (concentration_score × 0.35) = 1.0 + (0.45 × 0.35) = 1.1575. Data center vacancy <5% (CBRE 2024) but geographic concentration varies. Power constraints: N.Virginia 1.7% vacancy, Singapore 2.4%. Not monopolistic, but capacity-constrained.
correlation.note: Independence assumption would underestimate by ~15.7%. Prior under independence: 0.126 vs conditional: 0.1458.
correlation.concentration_rationale: Data center vacancy <5% (CBRE 2024) but geographic concentration varies. Power constraints: N.Virginia 1.7% vacancy, Singapore 2.4%. Not monopolistic, but capacity-constrained.

cascade_participation.note: Not part of documented cascade chains. Use unconditional probability.

North_America.basis: CHIPS Act investment creating domestic capacity. Intel, Samsung fabs under construction. US is the ORIGINATOR of export controls, not target.

China.basis: Direct target of export controls. Entity List restrictions. Domestic alternatives (SMIC) limited to mature nodes. Extreme concentration risk.

SE_Asia.basis: Assembly/test operations (Malaysia, Philippines) less affected by design restrictions. But still dependent on TSMC for advanced chips.

India.basis: Tata-PSMC, Micron assembly. Zero advanced node capability. 100% import dependency for <14nm chips.

LATAM.basis: No semiconductor manufacturing. Complete import dependency. Mexico auto sector: critical chip supply chain exposure.

Africa.basis: No semiconductor capacity. Last-priority allocation. Lead times longest globally during shortages.

geographic_adjustment.data_quality_note: Regional adjustments include event-specific factors where data supports differentiation (3/6 regions event-specific). Remaining regions use structural indicator estimates (World Bank LPI 2024, ASCE 2024).

correlation.mechanism: TSMC 90% advanced node monopoly means export controls are both more likely (single target) and more impactful (no alternatives). US BIS rounds (Oct 2022, Oct 2023, Dec 2024) each specifically targeted TSMC-dependent supply chains.
correlation.mathematical_relationship: Multiplier = 1.0 + (concentration_score × 0.35) = 1.0 + (0.92 × 0.35) = 1.322. TSMC 92% advanced node share = near-monopoly HHI≈8500. Zero short-term alternatives for <5nm. Export controls actively exploiting this concentration (3 rounds, 2022-2024).
correlation.note: Independence assumption would underestimate by ~32.2%. Prior under independence: 0.3128 vs conditional: 0.4136.
correlation.concentration_rationale: TSMC 64-66% overall foundry (IDC 2025), 90%+ at 3nm specifically (TrendForce). Samsung foundry 7.2% Q2 2025. At cutting-edge nodes relevant to AI/HPC, concentration approaches HHI=8000+. Three rounds of US export controls (2022-2024) demonstrate active weaponization of this dependency.

tail_risk.note: Semiconductor export controls expanded 3 times in 2 years (2022-2024), each round broader than expected. Additional controls on DRAM, HBM, and advanced packaging tools demonstrate escalation pattern. Industry consensus (SIA) consistently underestimated control scope.

cascade_participation.warning: This event participates in 1 cascade chain(s). Probability increases significantly if upstream events occur. Use conditional probabilities for scenario planning.

DIG-HWS-002.event_name: Semiconductor supply chain disruption (fab fire, earthquake)

North_America.basis: US fabs less exposed to seismic/typhoon than Taiwan/Japan. But Texas freeze (2021) showed weather vulnerability. Samsung Austin shutdown.

China.basis: Chinese fabs less vulnerable to earthquake (different geology) but subject to political disruption risk.

SE_Asia.basis: ASEAN semiconductor fabs in earthquake-prone zones (Philippines, Malaysia). Flooding risk in Thailand (2011 HDD crisis).

India.basis: Seismic zone IV-V in Gujarat (proposed fab locations). Monsoon flooding. But no existing fab infrastructure to disrupt.

LATAM.basis: Mexico earthquake risk (2017 precedent). Brazil: low natural disaster risk for semiconductor supply chain.

Africa.basis: Minimal fab infrastructure to disrupt. Supply chain disruption would be via import channel delays.

geographic_adjustment.data_quality_note: Regional adjustments include event-specific factors where data supports differentiation (2/6 regions event-specific). Remaining regions use structural indicator estimates (World Bank LPI 2024, ASCE 2024).

correlation.note: Semiconductor fab disruption is driven by natural disasters (earthquake, fire) and rare equipment failures. TSMC Kumamoto earthquake (2016), Samsung Austin freeze (2021), Renesas fire (2021) — trigger events are seismic/meteorological/accidental, not correlated with market dependency levels.

cascade_participation.note: Not part of documented cascade chains. Use unconditional probability.

DIG-HWS-003.event_name: Legacy chip / Mature node shortage (automotive MCUs)

North_America.basis: Texas Instruments, NXP, ON Semi domestic legacy node capacity. US has some mature node production.

China.basis: SMIC mature nodes growing. Government subsidizing 28nm+ production. But quality/yield gap with Taiwan/Japan for automotive MCUs.

SE_Asia.basis: Malaysia: legacy node testing/packaging. Singapore: GlobalFoundries 28nm. Some regional mature node access.

India.basis: Zero legacy chip production domestically. 100% import-dependent for automotive MCUs and industrial controllers.

LATAM.basis: No legacy chip production. Mexico auto sector depends entirely on imported MCUs.

Africa.basis: No chip production. Minimal electronics manufacturing. Import dependency total.

geographic_adjustment.data_quality_note: Event-specific regional adjustments based on infrastructure, regulatory, and market data cited in each basis field.

correlation.note: Legacy chip shortage is demand-driven (automotive chip cycles) not supply-structure-driven. 2021-2023 shortage was triggered by COVID demand whiplash + automotive rebound, not by changes in market concentration.

cascade_participation.note: Not part of documented cascade chains. Use unconditional probability.

DIG-HWS-004.event_name: Hardware supply chain attack / Compromised components

North_America.basis: NIST SP 800-161 supply chain risk management framework. DoD SCRM requirements. Strongest counterfeit detection infrastructure.

China.basis: Counterfeit component risk highest globally for sourcing FROM China (ERAI 2024). Testing infrastructure exists but governance gaps.

SE_Asia.basis: Shenzhen electronics markets: counterfeit component hub. Component brokers with variable quality control.

India.basis: Counterfeit component issue in industrial/automotive sectors. BIS certification improving but testing lab capacity limited.

LATAM.basis: Lower counterfeit risk than Asia but limited testing infrastructure. Mexico maquiladora sector: component provenance verification gaps.

Africa.basis: Highest counterfeit risk market. Limited testing infrastructure. WHO estimates 30% of pharmaceuticals counterfeit — electronics similar pattern.

geographic_adjustment.data_quality_note: Event-specific regional adjustments based on infrastructure, regulatory, and market data cited in each basis field.

correlation.note: Hardware supply chain attacks depend on adversary capability and access (state-sponsored groups, insider threats). Bloomberg SuperMicro reporting (2018, later disputed) showed detection is the variable, not market exposure.

cascade_participation.note: Not part of documented cascade chains. Use unconditional probability.

North_America.basis: Applied Materials, Lam Research, KLA domestic. Strongest equipment supply chain proximity. But ASML EUV from Netherlands.

China.basis: BIS equipment controls directly restrict. SMIC cannot access EUV. AMEC/Naura developing domestic alternatives but years behind.

SE_Asia.basis: Not direct consumers of most advanced equipment. Assembly/test equipment less restricted.

India.basis: No semiconductor equipment ecosystem. Would need to import everything for new fabs. Long lead times.

LATAM.basis: No semiconductor equipment market. Irrelevant for current manufacturing base.

Africa.basis: No semiconductor equipment market. Complete non-factor.

geographic_adjustment.data_quality_note: Event-specific regional adjustments based on infrastructure, regulatory, and market data cited in each basis field.

correlation.mechanism: ASML EUV monopoly (100% market share) and Applied Materials/Lam/TEL oligopoly in deposition/etch tools creates extreme supply fragility. Any disruption to these single-source suppliers cascades to entire semiconductor industry.
correlation.mathematical_relationship: Multiplier = 1.0 + (concentration_score × 0.35) = 1.0 + (0.82 × 0.35) = 1.287. ASML 100% EUV monopoly (HHI=10000). Applied/Lam/TEL oligopoly in deposition/etch (HHI≈3500). €36B backlog, 2+ year lead times.
correlation.note: Independence assumption would underestimate by ~28.7%. Prior under independence: 0.1196 vs conditional: 0.1539.
correlation.concentration_rationale: ASML 100% EUV monopoly (HHI=10000). Applied/Lam/TEL oligopoly in deposition/etch (HHI≈3500). €36B backlog, 2+ year lead times.

cascade_participation.note: Not part of documented cascade chains. Use unconditional probability.

DIG-HWS-006.event_name: Industrial automation hardware dependency / Vendor lock-in

North_America.basis: Rockwell, Honeywell domestic. Strongest industrial automation vendor proximity. But global supply chains for components.

China.basis: Siemens, ABB have China operations. Domestic alternatives (Inovance) growing but lagging in precision. Geopolitical sourcing risk.

SE_Asia.basis: Automation vendor presence growing (Singapore hub). But regional manufacturing still lower automation penetration.

India.basis: Growing automation market but vendor dependency on imports. L&T, Bharat Heavy limited domestic capability vs Siemens/ABB.

LATAM.basis: Mexico: auto sector well-served by global vendors. Brazil: moderate automation vendor presence.

Africa.basis: Minimal automation vendor presence. Service/support infrastructure limited. Lead times for parts/service 4-8 weeks.

geographic_adjustment.data_quality_note: Event-specific regional adjustments based on infrastructure, regulatory, and market data cited in each basis field.

correlation.note: Industrial automation hardware dependency relates to vendor lock-in but trigger events (price hikes, availability restrictions) depend on vendor-specific business decisions (M&A, product lifecycle) that are idiosyncratic to each vendor, not market-wide.

DIG-HWS-007.event_name: Machine tool / Manufacturing equipment access restriction

North_America.basis: US machine tool consumption 3rd globally. Domestic production (Haas, Gleason). But high-precision CNC imported from Japan/Germany.

China.basis: China: largest machine tool producer AND consumer. DMTG, Shenyang domestic. But high-precision still imported. Self-sufficiency growing.

SE_Asia.basis: Minimal domestic machine tool production (exception: Taiwan, technically). Import-dependent for CNC equipment.

India.basis: India machine tool industry small (IMTMA: $2B). HMT declining. Import dependency for precision equipment >60%.

LATAM.basis: Mexico auto sector uses imported machine tools. Brazil: some domestic production (Romi, Ergomat) but limited range.

Africa.basis: No meaningful machine tool production. Complete import dependency. Maintenance support infrastructure absent.

geographic_adjustment.data_quality_note: Event-specific regional adjustments based on infrastructure, regulatory, and market data cited in each basis field.

correlation.note: Machine tool access restrictions depend on export control policy decisions by specific governments (US BIS, Japan METI) — timing driven by geopolitical events, not by the degree of existing machine tool dependency.

DIG-SWS-001.event_name: Critical software license termination (ERP, CAD, OS)

China.basis: Sanctions risk: SAP, Oracle, Microsoft all exited Russia. Same could apply to China in escalation scenario. Kingdee/Yonyou alternatives exist.

SE_Asia.basis: Standard ERP market dynamics. SAP, Oracle well-established. Regional implementation partners available.

India.basis: TCS, Infosys provide implementation/support. SAP/Oracle dominant. India-specific ERPs (Tally, Busy) for SME segment.

EU.basis: Strongest ERP ecosystem. SAP headquartered in Germany. European data sovereignty provides some vendor stability.

LATAM.basis: TOTVS (Brazil) provides local alternative. SAP/Oracle dominant in enterprise. Localization requirements well-served.

Africa.basis: SAP Africa presence. But smaller market = lower vendor priority. Implementation partner depth limited outside South Africa.

geographic_adjustment.data_quality_note: Event-specific regional adjustments based on infrastructure, regulatory, and market data cited in each basis field.

correlation.mechanism: ERP market concentration (SAP/Oracle 60%+, Gartner 2024) creates deep dependency. ERP migrations average 17.4 months (Panorama 2024) making forced termination extremely disruptive.
correlation.mathematical_relationship: Multiplier = 1.0 + (concentration_score × 0.35) = 1.0 + (0.64 × 0.35) = 1.224. SAP+Oracle 60%+ ERP share (HHI≈2500). Migration: 17.4 months avg (Panorama 2024). Sanctions-triggered terminations demonstrated in Russia 2022.
correlation.note: Independence assumption would underestimate by ~22.4%. Prior under independence: 0.0883 vs conditional: 0.1081.
correlation.concentration_rationale: SAP+Oracle 60%+ ERP share (HHI≈2500). Migration: 17.4 months avg (Panorama 2024). Sanctions-triggered terminations demonstrated in Russia 2022.

DIG-SWS-002.event_name: Open-source maintainer risk / Supply chain failure (Log4j)

North_America.basis: Strongest OSS security ecosystem. OpenSSF, OSSF funded. Major tech companies invest in critical OSS maintenance.

China.basis: Gitee ecosystem. Chinese OSS contributions growing but maintainer fragility similar to global pattern.

SE_Asia.basis: OSS consumption high but contribution/maintenance support limited. Dependency without investment.

India.basis: India: 2nd largest GitHub contributor base. Strong OSS participation but similar maintainer fragility issues as global.

LATAM.basis: OSS consumption patterns similar to global. Limited maintainer presence in critical projects.

Africa.basis: OSS consumption growing with developer community. But contribution to critical projects minimal. No backstop for supply chain issues.

geographic_adjustment.data_quality_note: Event-specific regional adjustments based on infrastructure, regulatory, and market data cited in each basis field.

correlation.mechanism: OSS maintainer risk is structural: 25% of critical projects have 1-2 maintainers (OpenSSF Census III, 2024). As dependency deepens, maintainer burnout/compromise probability increases.
correlation.mathematical_relationship: Multiplier = 1.0 + (concentration_score × 0.35) = 1.0 + (0.49 × 0.35) = 1.1715. OpenSSF Census III, 2024: 25% critical projects have ≤2 maintainers. But core infrastructure (Linux kernel, OpenSSL) has dedicated funding. Risk concentrated in long-tail dependencies.
correlation.note: Independence assumption would underestimate by ~17.1%. Prior under independence: 0.2164 vs conditional: 0.2535.
correlation.concentration_rationale: OpenSSF Census III, 2024: 25% critical projects have ≤2 maintainers. But core infrastructure (Linux kernel, OpenSSL) has dedicated funding. Risk concentrated in long-tail dependencies.

China.basis: China AI regulation most restrictive. Generative AI registration required. Algorithmic recommendation governance. Baidu, Alibaba domestic alternatives.

SE_Asia.basis: AI regulation minimal. Singapore FEAT framework voluntary. ASEAN AI governance framework non-binding.

India.basis: India AI regulation light-touch so far. Digital India Act forthcoming. Large AI talent pool.

EU.basis: EU AI Act: most comprehensive AI regulation globally. High-risk classification creates compliance burden. But provides clarity.

LATAM.basis: AI regulation minimal. Brazil AI Bill in discussion. Mexico, Colombia: no specific AI frameworks.

Africa.basis: AI regulation nascent. AU AI Continental Strategy exists but no binding frameworks. Low current regulatory risk.
